figures.caption: Figure 1: Overview of AVR models. Top row: Conventional thinking VRMs tend to produce full reasoning paths regardless of task complexity. Bottom row: AVR decomposes visual reasoning into three cognitive functions and enables adaptive format selection.
Table 1: Performance of model variants across reasoning-intensive, perception-intensive, and general benchmarks. Bold indicates the best and underline indicates second best.
Figure 6: Training dynamics of FS-GRPO optimization on Qwen3-VL-2B. We report the mean reward and answer accuracy on both the training and validation sets.
Figure 3: Format distribution across different types of tasks using Qwen3-VL-4B. The hatched areas indicate the proportion of correct answers generated under each format.
Table 4: Composition of the RL training dataset. This diverse distribution enables the model to learn adaptive reasoning formats across tasks with different reasoning requirements.
Table 5: Cross-family evaluation on InternVL3 models. ∆ is computed with respect to the CoT baseline. Accuracy denotes absolute difference, and token reduction denotes relative percentage decrease.
Figure 5: Training dynamics of response format usage during RL on Qwen3-VL-2B.
